text: The robotic third arm is positioned on the left side of the patient and is utilized to retract the liver upwards, providing better exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a hook diathermy to dissect the anterior and posterior aspects of Calot's triangle, carefully separating the tissue in this region.
During the robotic cholecystectomy, the surgeon observes a markedly dilated cystic duct upon dissection.
The surgeon clips and divides a high-rising cystic artery using robotic instruments, visible within the surgical field.

Indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence imaging is used to visualize the anatomy of the dilated cystic duct, the common bile duct, and their junction, confirming the presence of stones within the cystic duct.
The surgeon achieves the critical view of safety and proceeds to dissect the gallbladder from the liver using robotic instruments.
The surgeon opens the dilated cystic duct and retrieves stones using upward stroke pressure applied to both the cystic duct and the common bile duct.
The surgeon uses a Fogarty catheter to remove debris from the cystic duct, extracting an additional stone that was impacted within the duct.
The surgeon shortens the cystic duct to facilitate the extraction of deeply placed stones, using precise cutting techniques to adjust the duct length.
The surgeon performs an intraoperative cholangiogram, injecting contrast to visualize and assess the patency of the common bile duct, confirming its anatomical structure and flow.
The surgeon closes the cystic duct stump using a continuous suture with 4-0 PDS, securing the duct after stone retrieval and dissection.
